name: wordpress-theme-development
description: WordPress theme development workflow covering theme architecture, template hierarchy, custom post types, block editor support, and responsive design.
capabilities: Guide through complete WordPress theme creation from setup to deployment
Implement WordPress template hierarchy with proper file structure
Integrate Gutenberg block editor support with custom blocks and patterns
Register custom post types and taxonomies for advanced content modeling
Configure theme functions including navigation menus and widget areas
Coordinate testing workflows for cross-browser and accessibility validation
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 40 potential issues (28 external_commands, 12 blocker patterns). All findings are false positives. The 'external_commands' detections are backtick characters in markdown code blocks used for skill invocation examples (e.g., @app-builder, @frontend-developer), not actual shell command executions. The 'blocker' patterns are matches on the word 'block' referring to Gutenberg editor blocks, not cryptographic weaknesses. This is a legitimate WordPress theme development workflow skill with no malicious intent or security risks.
valueStatement: Streamline WordPress theme development with a structured workflow covering theme architecture, template hierarchy, and Gutenberg block editor integration. Create responsive, accessible themes following WordPress best practices from scaffolding to testing.
userTitle: Build WordPress Themes with Modern Block Support
limitations: Requires existing WordPress installation and development environment
Does not execute WordPress CLI commands or generate code directly
Focused on classic and block themes, not headless WordPress implementations
Assumes familiarity with PHP, CSS, and basic WordPress concepts

useCases.title: Create Custom WordPress Theme
useCases.description: Design and build a unique WordPress theme from scratch with custom templates, styling, and block editor support for a client project.

useCases.title: Convert HTML Design to WordPress
useCases.description: Transform an existing HTML/CSS design into a fully functional WordPress theme with proper template hierarchy and dynamic content.

useCases.title: Add Block Editor Support
useCases.description: Modernize an existing WordPress theme by adding Gutenberg block support, custom block patterns, and block templates.

promptTemplates.title: Start New WordPress Theme
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create a new WordPress theme called '{theme_name}' with modern block editor support. Set up the basic directory structure, style.css with theme headers, and functions.php with essential theme support features.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginning a new WordPress theme project from scratch

promptTemplates.title: Create Custom Template Files
promptTemplates.prompt: Create WordPress template files for my theme including single.php, page.php, archive.php, and a custom template for '{custom_post_type}' posts. Follow WordPress template hierarchy best practices.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building template hierarchy for custom content display

promptTemplates.title: Register Custom Post Type
promptTemplates.prompt: Register a custom post type called '{post_type_slug}' with support for {features}. Include custom taxonomies and meta boxes, and create the corresponding archive template.
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding custom content types beyond posts and pages

promptTemplates.title: Add Gutenberg Block Support
promptTemplates.prompt: Add full Gutenberg block editor support to my WordPress theme. Enable block styles, register {number} custom blocks, and create block patterns for {use_case}.

bestPractices: Always use child themes when modifying existing WordPress themes to preserve changes during updates
Follow WordPress Coding Standards for PHP, HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to ensure compatibility
Implement proper template hierarchy using the correct file names and fallback templates
Test themes across multiple browsers and devices to ensure responsive design works correctly
antiPatterns: Avoid editing parent theme files directly - always use child themes or custom plugins for modifications
Never hardcode absolute URLs or paths - use WordPress functions like get_template_directory_uri() and home_url()
Do not use query_posts() or wp_reset_query() - use WP_Query or pre_get_posts filter instead
Avoid deregistering core WordPress scripts or styles unless absolutely necessary for functionality

faq.question: What is the difference between a WordPress theme and a plugin?
faq.answer: Themes control how your website looks and handles frontend display, while plugins add functionality and features. Themes contain template files and assets, plugins contain PHP functions and logic. Use themes for presentation, plugins for functionality.

faq.question: Should I use a classic theme or a block theme?
faq.answer: Block themes are the modern standard and fully support the Gutenberg editor with theme.json and block templates. Classic themes use PHP templates and may have limited block support. Choose block themes for new projects to take advantage of full-site editing.

faq.question: Do I need to know PHP to create WordPress themes?
faq.answer: Yes, PHP knowledge is essential for theme development. You need PHP to create template files, register theme features, enqueue assets, and interact with WordPress APIs. Basic PHP skills are sufficient for simple themes, advanced themes require deeper understanding.

faq.question: How do I ensure my theme works with page builders?
faq.answer: Most page builders work with any properly coded WordPress theme. Ensure your theme has proper wrapper markup, uses WordPress CSS classes, and does not interfere with content filters. Test with your specific page builder to verify compatibility.

faq.question: Can I use CSS frameworks like Bootstrap or Tailwind with WordPress themes?
faq.answer: Yes, you can integrate any CSS framework. Enqueue the framework files in functions.php and override default WordPress styles. Be aware that some frameworks may conflict with WordPress core styles or block editor output, so test thoroughly.

faq.question: How do I make my theme accessible and WCAG compliant?
faq.answer: Follow WordPress accessibility guidelines: use semantic HTML, proper heading hierarchy, ARIA labels, keyboard navigation support, and sufficient color contrast. Test with accessibility tools and ensure all interactive elements are keyboard accessible. WordPress core provides many accessibility features you should leverage.
